perturbation.original_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties shall not, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined) or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties may, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined). However, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, they shall not, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date.
perturbation.explanation: The initial text explicitly prohibits the Seller Parties from engaging in the Company's Business during the Restricted Period. The modified text introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that the Seller Parties may directly or indirectly engage in The Company's Business, but then includes a restriction on owning interest or participating in entities that compete with the Company's Business. This creates ambiguity as to whether the Seller Parties can directly compete or not, especially considering the original agreement's strict non-compete clause. The contradiction arises because one section states that the Seller Parties 'may' engage in the Company's Business, whereas other provisions restrict competitive activities.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For a period of four years from the date of this Agreement (the "Restricted Period"), Seller Parties may, directly or indirectly, engage in The Company's Business (as hereinafter defined). However, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, they shall not, directly or indirectly, own an interest in, manage, operate, join, control, lend money or render financial or other assistance to or participate in, as a partner, stockholder, consultant or otherwise, any Person that competes with The Company's Business as it is conducted as of the Closing Date. For purposes of this Agreement, the term the "The Company's Business" shall mean any business anywhere in the world that provides products or services of the kind provided by the Business (as defined in the Recitals of the Asset Purchase Agreement) as of the Closing Date. Notwithstanding the foregoing, for the purposes of this Section 1, ownership of securities having no more than one percent (1%) of the outstanding voting power of any competitor which are listed on any national securities exchange, automated securities exchange (including Nasdaq) or traded actively in the national over-the-counter market (in each case including any foreign equivalent) shall not be deemed to be in violation of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text established a clear prohibition on Seller Parties engaging in The Company's Business during the Restricted Period.  The changed text introduces a direct contradiction by stating that Seller Parties *may* engage in The Company's Business. While it then adds a restriction on competing activities, this creates a significant ambiguity. The Contradicted Text mirrors the changed text, highlighting the core issue: the 'may engage' clause directly conflicts with the original intent of a complete non-compete, creating an inconsistency that a reasonable reader would immediately recognize as incompatible.  The explanation clearly points out that the 'may' introduces a direct conflict with the original 'shall not' clause, making enforcement unclear and potentially unenforceable. This satisfies the criteria for a strong in-text contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period and at all times thereafter, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties. However, after the Restricted Period, Seller Parties are only expected to safeguard Confidential Information to the best of their ability, and minor, unintentional disclosures will not be considered a breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates that the Seller Parties must maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information 'at all times thereafter'. The modified text introduces an in-text contradiction by relaxing this requirement after the Restricted Period, implying a lower standard of care is acceptable and allowing minor unintentional disclosures. This creates a contradiction between the obligation to maintain strict confidence at all times, and a later relaxed standard of safeguarding the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties will keep and hold all Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined) in strict confidence, and will not use or disclose in any way any of such Confidential Information without the prior express written consent of the Buyer Parties. However, after the Restricted Period, Seller Parties are only expected to safeguard Confidential Information to the best of their ability, and minor, unintentional disclosures will not be considered a breach of this agreement. For the purposes of this Agreement, the term "Confidential Information" shall mean all trade secrets and confidential or proprietary information (and any tangible representation thereof) owned, possessed or used in connection with The Company Business or by the Buyer Parties and its Affiliates; provided, however, that "Confidential Information" does not include information which is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by a Seller Party..
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes an unconditional obligation for Seller Parties to maintain strict confidentiality of Confidential Information 'at all times thereafter'.  The changed text directly contradicts this by introducing a lower standard of care after the Restricted Period, explicitly permitting 'minor, unintentional disclosures'. This creates a clear and direct conflict. The Contradicted Text mirrors the Changed Text, highlighting the contradiction. The original text and the Contradicted Text both demand strict confidentiality at all times, while the Changed Text weakens this requirement after the Restricted Period, making enforcement unclear and creating a logical inconsistency.  A reasonable reader would immediately recognize the incompatibility between the unconditional obligation of the original and contradicted text and the relaxed standard introduced in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do, or attempt to do, business with any customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date (provided that Simtek shall have the right to do business with any of its current or former customers), or interfere with or attempt to interfere with any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, induce or attempt to induce any of them to leave the employ of the Buyer Parties or violate the terms of their Contracts, or any employment arrangements, with Buyer Parties, or, without the prior written consent of Buyer Parties, directly or indirectly, solicit to hire, hire or employ, whether as an employee, director, contractor, consultant or otherwise any officers, employees or consultants of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date, or interfere with any officers of the Buyer Parties, or solicit to hire any officers of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement. However, the Seller Parties are allowed to advise customers of the Company and interfere with consultants of the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original non-solicitation clause prevents Seller Parties from soliciting or advising customers of the Company, interfering with officers, employees, or consultants of the Buyer Parties. The modification introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that 'the Seller Parties are allowed to advise customers of the Company and interfere with consultants of the Buyer Parties'. This directly contradicts the initial restrictions within the same section, creating an internal inconsistency that could undermine the clause's enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Restricted Period, Seller Parties agree that they will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for the purpose of conducting or engaging in any business that competes with The Company Business, call upon customers of the Company engaged prior to the Closing Date, or interfere with any officers of the Buyer Parties, or solicit to hire any officers of the Buyer Parties, unless such Person's employment is terminated by the Seller Parties or its Affiliate; provided, however, that this Section 3 shall not prohibit general solicitations of or advertisements for employment by the Seller Parties that are not generally directed at any officers, employees, representatives or agents of the Buyer Parties, so long as the Seller Parties do not hire any such Person who responds to any such general solicitation or advertisement. However, the Seller Parties are allowed to advise customers of the Company and interfere with consultants of the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows the Seller Parties to "advise customers of the Company and interfere with consultants of the Buyer Parties." This directly contradicts the original text and the contradicted text, which explicitly prohibit these actions.  The original text states that Seller Parties will not "call upon, solicit, advise or otherwise do business with any customers... or interfere with... consultants of the Buyer Parties." The modification introduces a direct and clear conflict, creating an internal inconsistency within Section 3. This inconsistency makes the clause's enforceability unclear and would likely cause confusion and potential legal disputes. The change is not a minor alteration; it fundamentally alters the scope of the non-solicitation and non-interference restrictions.  Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by the Seller Parties and the Buyer Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by at least one of the Seller Parties and one of the Buyer Parties. However, immaterial modifications may be implemented via email correspondence between any authorized representative of each party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that amendments require a written instrument signed by all Seller Parties and Buyer Parties. The modified clause introduces a contradiction by allowing for immaterial modifications via email correspondence between any authorized representative, relaxing the signature requirement and opening the door for disputes over what constitutes an 'immaterial modification'. This is a direct contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement may not be amended, modified or supplemented by the parties hereto in any manner, except by an instrument in writing signed by at least one of the Seller Parties and one of the Buyer Parties. However, immaterial modifications may be implemented via email correspondence between any authorized representative of each party. -2- <PAGE> 7. No Waiver. The terms and conditions of this Agreement may be waived only by a written instrument signed by the party waiving compliance. The failure of any party hereto to enforce at any time any of the provisions of this Agreement shall in no way be construed to be a waiver of any such provision, nor in any way to affect the validity of this Agreement or any part hereof or the right of such party thereafter to enforce each and every such provision. No waiver of any breach of or non-compliance with this Agreement shall be held to be a waiver of any other or subsequent breach or non-compliance. The rights and remedies herein provided are cumulative and are not exclusive of any rights or remedies that any party may otherwise have at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a strict requirement for amendments: written instrument signed by all Seller and Buyer Parties.  The changed text introduces a direct contradiction by allowing immaterial modifications via email correspondence between any authorized representative of each party. This relaxes the signature requirement significantly, creating a clear conflict. The 'Contradicted Text' section, which is identical to the changed text, further highlights this contradiction.  The explanation correctly points out that the ambiguity around 'immaterial modification' opens the door for disputes, making enforcement unclear and creating a strong, direct contradiction to the original, stricter requirement. The original text and the changed text, when compared, show a clear shift from a requirement of signatures from all parties to a less stringent requirement, directly contradicting the principle of requiring written consent from all parties as stated in the original text.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement may be waived only by a written instrument signed by the party waiving compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement may be waived only by a written instrument signed by the party waiving compliance. However, consistent inaction regarding a breach shall constitute an implicit waiver of that specific term.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly requires a 'written instrument' for any waiver. The modified text introduces an in-text contradiction by suggesting that 'consistent inaction regarding a breach shall constitute an implicit waiver', thereby contradicting the need for a written instrument. This contradiction creates uncertainty about how waivers can occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The terms and conditions of this Agreement may be waived only by a written instrument signed by the party waiving compliance. However, consistent inaction regarding a breach shall constitute an implicit waiver of that specific term. The failure of any party hereto to enforce at any time any of the provisions of this Agreement shall in no way be construed to be a waiver of any such provision, nor in any way to affect the validity of this Agreement or any part hereof or the right of such party thereafter to enforce each and every such provision. No waiver of any breach of or non-compliance with this Agreement shall be held to be a waiver of any other or subsequent breach or non-compliance. The rights and remedies herein provided are cumulative and are not exclusive of any rights or remedies that any party may otherwise have at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes that waivers require a written, signed instrument. The changed text introduces a contradictory clause stating that 'consistent inaction regarding a breach shall constitute an implicit waiver'. This directly conflicts with the requirement for a written instrument, creating a clear and strong contradiction.  The contradicted section reinforces the need for a written waiver, further highlighting the incompatibility introduced by the change. The change creates uncertainty regarding the method of waiver, making enforcement unclear and potentially unenforceable.  A reasonable reader would immediately recognize the incompatibility between the requirement for a written waiver and the allowance of an implicit waiver through inaction.

perturbation.original_text: Seller Parties recognize that money damages alone would not adequately compensate Buyer Parties and its Affiliates in the event of breach by Seller Parties of this Agreement, and Seller Parties therefore agree that, in addition to all other remedies available to Buyer Parties and its Affiliates, at law, in equity or otherwise, Buyer Parties and its Affiliates shall be entitled to injunctive relief for the enforcement hereof. All rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and are in addition to and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies available, at law, in equity, by agreement or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller Parties recognize that money damages alone may not adequately compensate Buyer Parties and its Affiliates in the event of breach by Seller Parties of this Agreement, and Seller Parties therefore agree that, in addition to all other remedies available to Buyer Parties and its Affiliates, at law, in equity or otherwise, Buyer Parties and its Affiliates may be entitled to injunctive relief for the enforcement hereof. All rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and are in addition to and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies available, at law, in equity, by agreement or otherwise. However, the amount of money damages shall be capped at 10% of the Purchase Price.
perturbation.explanation: The original text says Buyer Parties 'shall be entitled to injunctive relief', implying it is guaranteed. The modified text changes this to 'may be entitled to injunctive relief' introducing an element of discretion. This is a contradiction. Further the last addition also introduces contradictory financial compensation guidelines that did not exist before.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller Parties recognize that money damages alone may not adequately compensate Buyer Parties and its Affiliates in the event of breach by Seller Parties of this Agreement, and Seller Parties therefore agree that, in addition to all other remedies available to Buyer Parties and its Affiliates, at law, in equity or otherwise, Buyer Parties and its Affiliates may be entitled to injunctive relief for the enforcement hereof. All rights and remedies hereunder are cumulative and are in addition to and not exclusive of any other rights and remedies available, at law, in equity, by agreement or otherwise. However, the amount of money damages shall be capped at 10% of the Purchase Price.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Buyer Parties 'shall be entitled to injunctive relief,' indicating a guaranteed right. The changed text alters this to 'may be entitled to injunctive relief,' introducing uncertainty and discretion.  This directly contradicts the original text's clear assertion of entitlement.  Furthermore, the addition of a cap on money damages ('However, the amount of money damages shall be capped at 10% of the Purchase Price') conflicts with the original text's premise that money damages alone are inadequate compensation and that injunctive relief is necessary. The contradicted section mirrors the changed text, highlighting the contradiction introduced by the modification. The change creates a clear and direct conflict, making enforcement unclear and introducing a significant inconsistency within the document.
